exemplar.title:
How varying the diameter of a copper tube affects the time it takes a
neodymium magnet to fall through it?

How varying the diameter of a copper tube affects the time it takes a
neodymium magnet to fall through it?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are labelled with units and include error bars
Uncertainties are appropriately propagated and reflected in gradient analysis
Processing (averages, linearisation, correlation) is carried out accurately
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation through logarithmic transformation is omitted
Some graphs lack trendâline equations and RÂ² values
Minor formatting issues in table headers reduce readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Recording and processing are both clear and precise, meeting the 5â6 band descriptor. Treatment of uncertainties is generally appropriate, though propagation through log transforms and full graph annotations are incomplete. These minor omissions justify a 5/6 rather than a perfect score.

strengths: Research question is explicitly stated within a focused theoretical context
Clear apparatus list and methodological outline support reproducibility
Controls and justification of key variables are well identified
weaknesses: Omission of essential operational details (magnet field strength, calibration specifics)
Derivation step lacks sign-convention justification and variable definitions
Minor digressions in the introduction limit flawless context setting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student situates the research question within a specific electromagnetic context and provides sufficient methodological detail to allow reproduction with few ambiguities. Controls and theoretical framing are well explained, aligning with the lower end of the 5â6 band. However, missing precise calibration parameters and some derivational ambiguities prevent a perfect score, warranting a 5/6.

strengths: Conclusion acknowledges that the initial linear hypothesis was incorrect
References to Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws provide relevant scientific context
weaknesses: Logical bridge between the exponential fit and theoretical expectations is only partially developed
Comparisons to accepted scientific values are qualitative and lack quantitative validation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and mostly consistent with the analysis, fitting the 3â4 band. Scientific comparisons are relevant but superficial, lacking quantitative depth. This performance merits a mid-band score of 3/6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified and relative impact is discussed
Improvements proposed (more trials, vacuum conditions, precision tubing) are realistic and relevant
weaknesses: Relative impact of some error sources is not quantitatively assessed
Descriptions of how improvements mitigate weaknesses lack explanatory detail
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific limitations and suggests realistic improvements, aligning with the 3â4 band. The lack of quantitative analysis of error impacts and deeper explanation of how each improvement addresses these shortcomings prevents top-band performance, resulting in a 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated and the student embeds it within a focused discussion of Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws, clarifying why copper tubes and a neodymium magnet are suitable for probing electromagnetic induction.  The background narrative explains how flux, emf and tube diameter interact, giving the investigation a clear, discipline-specific setting rather than a merely general one.  This specific theoretical framing satisfies the descriptor for a âspecific and appropriate context,â although minor digressions in the introduction prevent a flawless exposition, so the strand is best credited toward the lower end of the 5â6 band.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted for radius and timing; these are propagated to area, displayed as error bars and reflected in gradient uncertainty.  The discussion also cites percentage uncertainty of the gradient.  Although propagation through the log transformation is not fully elaborated, the treatment remains generally appropriate, meriting placement in the top band.

The student identifies and justifies key controls (magnet type, tube material/length, drop height) and selects a reasonably wide diameter range with five repeats, demonstrating awareness of relevance and sufficiency.  However, the rationale for only five radii and the small total data set is not fully explored, nor are secondary effects (eddy-current heating, magnetic coupling length) discussed.  This descriptiveânot fully âexplainedââtreatment fits comfortably in the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses are identifiedâtiming resolution, tube imperfections, air resistanceâand the student singles out timing as the largest contributor, indicating relative impact.  The explanation, however, lacks quantitative assessment and omits discussion of limited data breadth, preventing a top-band award; thus a solid 3-4 band placement (score 4) is appropriate.

The apparatus list, dimensions of each tube, video-analysis software and five repeated trials are all stated, enabling a competent reader to reproduce the core procedure.  Nevertheless, some essential operational details are missing (exact magnet mass/field strength, camera distance, frame calibration and precise start/stop markers).  These omissions create a few ambiguities, so the description aligns with âreproduced with few ambiguities,â placing it in the 3-4 band.

Averages, linearisation, gradient, intercept and correlation coefficient are all calculated correctly and applied in subsequent discussion.  No major computational errors are evident, and data transformation is suitable for model testing, so processing can be regarded as accurate and appropriate, warranting a 5 within the highest band.

Improvements such as more trials, wider diameter range, vacuum conditions and higher-precision tubing are realistic and relevant, yet the student stops at stating/describing them without fully explaining how each would specifically mitigate the identified weaknesses or affect data quality quantitatively.  This corresponds to the lower end of the 3-4 band (score 3).

Raw and processed data are tabulated with headings, units and uncertainties, while graphs are labelled and include error bars and trend lines.  Apart from minor formatting issues, the presentation is both legible and quantitatively precise, fulfilling the dual requirement of clarity and precision characteristic of the 5-6 band.

Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws are cited to explain the trend, establishing relevant scientific context; however, the comparison remains qualitative and lacks deeper quantitative validation (e.g., predicted terminal velocity or induced emf magnitude).  The treatment is therefore âsome relevant comparison,â meriting a mid-band score.

The student concludes that the original hypothesis of a simple linear relationship was incorrect and interprets the fitted exponential/linearised model with reference to gradient and intercept.  The argument is mostly consistent with the analysed data, yet the logical bridge from exponential behaviour back to Faradayâs law is only partially developed, and precision/accuracy comments are superficial.  This aligns with âdescribed but not fully consistent,â positioning it in the 3-4 band (score 3).

annotations.comment: Include essential method detailsâsuch as magnet field strength, dropâheight calibration and software settingsâin the abstract to enhance reproducibility.
The theoretical section lacks a quantitative derivation of the expected $$dt\propto1/A$$ relationship before proceeding to experimental design.
The explanation of Lenzâs law should explicitly connect how changes in magnetic flux rate translate into the observed time delay, clarifying underlying assumptions.
In the discussion of Fig. 2, the narrative refers to âupperâ and âlower coilsâ without specifying coil count or geometry; clarify these details to support the cancellation argument.
Axes in Fig. 2 should include units and clear labels for emf and time to ensure communication of data processing is both clear and precise.
The derivation from $$\varepsilon=-N\frac{d\phi}{dt}$$ to $$dt=\frac{-NB\,dA}{\varepsilon}$$ omits signâconvention justification and variable definitions; expand the rationale.
The research question is prominently and specifically stated in the title, providing a clear focus within the context of electromagnetic induction and appropriate theoretical framing.
The introduction would benefit from immediately situating the research question within realâworld applications or recent studies to strengthen the context and relevance.
The abstract omits concise numerical results and uncertainty values, limiting its utility for quickly assessing key findings and their reliability.
Control variables omit tube wall thickness and magnet field strength calibration; include these to ensure all factors affecting emf are managed.
Provide details on videoâanalysis calibrationâsuch as distance markers and frame rate verificationâto allow exact reproduction of timing measurements.
Table 1 presents multiple trials with uncertainties, demonstrating clear recording and processing of raw data.
In Table 1, combine units and uncertainties in column headers reduces readability; separate the unit notation from the uncertainty expression.
Table 2 correctly shows propagated uncertainties for area and time, illustrating thoughtful consideration of measurement error.
Table 3 retains original area uncertainties after log transformation, underestimating error; recalculate uncertainties in the log domain.
Define each variable and constant in the derivation step to remove ambiguity and aid replication of the mathematical model.
The hypothesis is clearly stated with an expected negative linear relationship and includes a quantitative intercept uncertainty, demonstrating strong methodological planning.
The text describes a âconstantly rising lineâ but the expected slope is negative; correct this to âfalling lineâ to align verbal and mathematical descriptions.
The uncertainty propagation through the logarithmic transformation is not shown; include a brief calculation or reference to a propagation formula.
In Fig. 4, adding the trendâline equation and an RÂ² value directly on the graph would improve quantitative clarity.
Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 are wellâlabelled and include analysis lines, facilitating clear and precise data communication.
Fig. 6 omits error bars on data points, limiting the visual assessment of scatter and precision relative to the model.
The conclusion states the method is âgood qualityâ based on 12.5% uncertainty without comparing to accepted experimental standards; contextualize uncertainty magnitude.
Relate the fitted slope and intercept back to values predicted by Faradayâs law quantitatively to strengthen the conclusionâs consistency with theory.
Evaluation lists sources of error but does not quantify their relative impacts; include a sensitivity analysis to explain which factor dominates.
For proposed improvements like vacuum trials, explain how reduced air resistance quantitatively influences time measurements to justify the suggestion.
The improvement to increase trial count is realistic but would benefit from specifying how many additional trials will reduce uncertainty (e.g., target standard error).

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
